questions.question: Which are the five major functional sections of ICS?
options: A. Command, Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Administration
B. Triage, Treatment, Transport, Staging, Morgue
C. Police, Fire, EMS, Hospital, Public Health
D. Detection, Safety, Support, Evacuation, Recovery
questions.rationale: ICS organizes all incident work into Command, Operations, Planning, Logistics, and Finance/Administration.

questions.question: Field triage, treatment, and transport belong to which ICS section?
questions.rationale: Operations performs the tactical, hands-on work of the response, including all patient-care activities.

questions.question: Obtaining ventilators, food for responders, and portable radios is the job of:
questions.rationale: Logistics acquires the resources the response needs — supplies, facilities, communications, and responder support.

questions.question: What is the recommended ICS span of control?
questions.rationale: One supervisor should directly manage roughly 3–7 people, classically 5. Larger spans trigger subdivision of the structure.

questions.question: A hazmat incident involves fire, police, EMS, and an environmental agency, each with legal authority. The best command arrangement is:
options: B. Unified command — agencies share objectives under one plan with a single Operations section
C. Whoever arrived first commands alone forever
D. No command until the state arrives
questions.rationale: Unified command lets multiple jurisdictions/disciplines jointly command while preserving one coordinated plan — the fix for competing command posts.

questions.question: Which ICS role can immediately halt an unsafe operation?
questions.rationale: The safety officer, part of the command staff, holds authority to stop any operation posing imminent danger.

questions.question: What is NIMS?
options: B. The national doctrine standardizing incident management — ICS, resource typing, credentialing, mutual aid, EOCs
questions.rationale: NIMS standardizes how all agencies and levels of government manage incidents so systems interoperate — ICS is its on-scene management component.

questions.question: During a hospital disaster activation, a nurse receives conflicting instructions from her everyday manager and her assigned incident supervisor. Per ICS, she should:
options: B. Follow her assigned incident supervisor — unity of command
C. Follow whichever instruction is easier
questions.rationale: ICS unity of command means each person reports to exactly one incident supervisor during the response, regardless of daily reporting lines.

questions.question: What must occur when command is transferred to a more senior officer?
options: B. A formal briefing of the incoming commander and an announcement to all
D. Approval from the hospital CEO
questions.rationale: Command transfer requires a briefing (situation, resources, plan) and notification so every responder knows who now commands.

questions.question: The incident action plan is produced by which section?
questions.rationale: Planning collects information, tracks resources and the situation, and prepares the incident action plan for command approval.

questions.question: An Emergency Operations Center (EOC) primarily:
options: A. Replaces the on-scene incident commander
B. Coordinates off-scene support — resources, information, and policy — for incident command
D. Is only activated for federal disasters
questions.rationale: The EOC supports and coordinates from off-scene; it does not take over tactical command of the incident.

questions.question: In hospital incident command (e.g., HICS), a job action sheet is:
options: B. A role card defining the incident duties, reporting line, and tasks for an assigned position
questions.rationale: Job action sheets translate the ICS structure into concrete role instructions so staff can step into incident positions quickly.

questions.question: A treatment group supervisor now directly manages 12 providers and is overwhelmed. The ICS-correct fix is:
options: B. Subdivide — create additional units/leaders to restore a 3–7 span of control
D. Ignore it — span of control is advisory
questions.rationale: ICS is modular: when span of control exceeds ~7, the structure subdivides so every supervisor manages a workable number.

questions.question: Why does ICS insist on common terminology (plain language, no codes)?
options: B. So responders from different agencies understand each other without translation errors
D. To comply with billing rules
questions.rationale: Agencies have different internal codes; plain, common terminology prevents dangerous misunderstandings in multi-agency operations.

questions.question: Which statement about ICS scalability is TRUE?
options: A. Every incident must activate all five sections fully
B. ICS expands and contracts modularly — small incidents may need only an incident commander
C. ICS applies only to incidents with over 100 casualties
questions.rationale: ICS is modular: functions are activated only as needed, growing with the incident and demobilizing as it resolves.

questions.question: A physician arrives at a hospital disaster and begins working wherever she sees need, without checking in. The ICS problem is:
options: A. None — initiative is always good
B. No check-in means no accountability, no assigned supervisor, and broken resource tracking
C. Physicians are exempt from ICS
D. She should have gone to the EOC
questions.rationale: Check-in establishes accountability and puts responders into the structure with a defined role and supervisor; unassigned helpers are untracked and can be unsafe.
